title: FPGA Based Feedforward System for Photonic Quantum Computing Applications
authors: Daniel Duggan, Simon Filgis, Axel B. Bregnsbo, Jürgen Saalmüller, Jonas S. Neergaard-Nielsen, Tobias Wintermantel, Ulrik L. Andersen
abstract: Field-programmable gate arrays provide a high-performance solution for real-time signal processing in emerging quantum and photonic technologies. We present an FPGA-based fast feedforward system, that incorporates a high quantum efficiency fully fibre based homodyne detector, to enable low-latency signal processing critical for continuous variables (CV) measurement-based quantum information processing (MB-QIP) protocols. CV MB-QIP typically relies on adaptive measurements and/or displacements via feedforward to achieve scalability and universality, but existing implementations typically handle these operations in post-processing, limiting real-time applicability. Our system performs signal acquisition, conditioning, and logic operations in real-time, meeting the tight latency requirements of photonic quantum computing protocols. The detector exhibits a large clearance of 15 dB at 1 GHz with 4 mW linear oscillator and quantum efficiencies of >95% with a total system latency of 196 ns. This work highlights the role of FPGAs in bridging the gap between theoretical models and physical implementations in photonics-based technologies
summary: This paper presents an FPGA-based fast feedforward system designed to enable real-time signal processing for Continuous Variable Measurement-Based Quantum Information Processing. By integrating a high-efficiency homodyne detector with low-latency digital electronics, the system overcomes the limitations of post-processing, allowing for deterministic, real-time quantum gate operations in photonic quantum computing.
main_problem: The core challenge is enabling low-latency, real-time signal processing required for Continuous Variables (CV) Measurement-Based Quantum Information Processing (MB-QIP) protocols.
main_result: The authors successfully implemented and demonstrated an FPGA-based fast feedforward system capable of performing necessary signal conditioning and logic operations in real-time with a total system latency of 196 ns.
method: The system uses an FPGA to process continuous measurement outcomes from a homodyne detector, performing real-time feedforward corrections necessary to maintain the quantum state during measurement-based computation.
model_or_system: The system is built around a high quantum efficiency, fully fibre-based homodyne detector coupled to an FPGA. This platform processes CV observables ($\hat{x}, \hat{p}$) derived from quantum harmonic oscillator models, which are central to CV MB-QIP.
key_observables: Quadrature measurements ($\hat{x}, \hat{p}$), system latency (196 ns), detector clearance (15 dB at 1 GHz), and phase output oscillation patterns.
important_parameters: Quantum efficiencies $> 95\%$, total system latency of 196 ns, and the need to correct deterministic displacements in phase space.
main_assumptions: The primary assumption is that the required feedforward corrections can be calculated and applied faster than the measurement rate, bridging the gap between theory and physical implementation.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the system architecture (FPGA components, data flow), the performance of the homodyne detector (clearance vs. frequency), and simulated/measured phase output oscillations confirming real-time operation.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the theoretical need for real-time feedforward in CV MB-QIP, details the physical components (homodyne detector, FPGA), describes the implementation of the feedforward calculation (inner products, CORDIC), and concludes with experimental verification of the low-latency performance.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it provides a concrete, high-speed engineering solution (FPGA) to a critical bottleneck—real-time control—in the experimental realization of photonic quantum computation protocols.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Standing wave inside a cavity with perfectly reflecting mirrors located at z = 0 and z = L. The wave is polarised in the x-direction.
Fig. 2: Wigner functions for vacuum, coherent (α = 2), squeezed vacuum, and single-photon states. The single-photon state is non-Gaussian and has a negative Wigner function.

Fig. 5: Concept of Gaussian Boson sampling. Squeezed vac- uum states enter on the left hand side, go through a series of phase shifters and beamsplitters (BS) that model a unitary U, and are detected on the right hand side by photon number resolving detectors (PNRD). In the original Boson Sampling proposal, the inputs are single photon states as opposed to Gaussian states.
Fig. 4: Wigner functions showing subsystem A before and after ˆx-measurement on subsystem B. Homodyne detection displaces A in phase space, requiring real-time correction.

Fig. 6: Schematic of the MB-GBS. A 2-D cluster state is generated via squeezed light and a system of delay lines and beamsplitters. Modes B are measured, inducing displacements in modes A, which enter a delay line to allow for feedforward- ing to occur. This is achieved via the FPGA-based feedforward system.
